content:
Execute the improvement plan at tools/title-loop/docs/2-072e8fb/improvement-plan.md.

Modify lib/text-classifier/title-extractor.ts and any related files as needed.
Make sure:
1. All existing tests still pass
2. The CLI at tools/title-loop/extract-title.ts still works
3. The code is clean and follows the project conventions in AGENTS.md
4. No unnecessary changes outside the title extraction logic

After making changes, run: npx vitest run lib/text-classifier/__tests__/title-extractor.test.ts
Fix any test failures before finishing.

content.thinking: Let me start by reading the improvement plan and understanding the current state of the code.

content.content:
1→# Improvement Plan — Iteration 3
     2→
     3→## Summary
     4→
     5→Four changes targeting the four systematic failure patterns from iteration 2. The changes are ordered by impact, with the first two fixes addressing the most failures. Expected impact: 5/5 failing cases fixed (1/6 → 6/6).
     6→
     7→All changes are in `lib/text-classifier/title-extractor.ts`. No new dependencies, no additional embedding calls. Total added complexity is O(n) string operations on ≤25 candidates — well within the 10-second mobile budget.
     8→
     9→---
    10→
    11→## Change 1: Invert Substring Deduplication
    12→
    13→**Addresses:** Failures #1 (ARAYES SHRAK), #2 (Baked Eggs)
    14→
    15→### Root cause
    16→
    17→Lines 281–289 deduplicate by removing any candidate whose text **contains** a shorter candidate's text. This is backwards: when both "ARAYES" (single line) and "ARAYES SHRAK" (2-line join) are candidates, the longer correct title is discarded in favour of the incomplete fragment.
    18→
    19→For failure #2, the same bug discards "Baked Eggs with Feta, Harissa Tomato Sauce & Coriander" (full join) because it contains "Baked Eggs with Feta, Harissa Tomato Sauce" (single line). Both the partial and the orphaned "& Coriander" then survive as separate candidates, producing the spurious `" + "` join.
    20→
    21→### Specific code change
    22→
    23→```typescript
    24→// BEFORE (lines 281-289) — removes the LONGER candidate:
    25→selected = selected.filter((a) => {
    26→  const aLower = a.text.toLowerCase();
    27→  return !selected.some(
    28→    (b) =>
    29→      b !== a &&
    30→      aLower.includes(b.text.toLowerCase()) &&
    31→      b.text.length < a.text.length
    32→  );
    33→});
    34→
    35→// AFTER — removes the SHORTER candidate (substring):
    36→selected = selected.filter((a) => {
    37→  const aLower = a.text.toLowerCase();
    38→  return !selected.some(
    39→    (b) =>
    40→      b !== a &&
    41→      b.text.toLowerCase().includes(aLower) &&
    42→      b.text.length > a.text.length
    43→  );
    44→});
    45→```
    46→
    47→The condition changes from "remove `a` if `a` contains a shorter `b`" to "remove `a` if a longer `b` contains `a`". The more complete title always wins.
    48→
    49→### Expected impact
    50→
    51→| Case | Before | After |
    52→|------|--------|-------|
    53→| ARAYES SHRAK | "ARAYES" wins (longer join discarded) | "ARAYES SHRAK" wins (shorter fragment discarded) |
    54→| Baked Eggs | Partial + "& Coriander" survive → joined with " + " | Full title wins; partial and fragment both discarded as substrings |
    55→
    56→---
    57→
    58→## Change 2: Reduce Position Bonus from Additive to Multiplicative
    59→
    60→**Addresses:** Failures #3 (FINNISH MILK FLATBREADS), #4 (MIXED SEED CRISPBREAD), #5 (SAFFRON WHEAT BUNS)
    61→
    62→### Root cause
    63→
    64→The additive position bonus of up to +0.15 is large enough to overwhelm semantic similarity scores. MiniLM cosine similarities for recipe-adjacent text typically range 0.05–0.20, so a +0.15 additive bonus at position 0 can double the effective score of a garbled early fragment and beat a legitimate title that appears later.
    65→
    66→All three failures share this pattern: garbled OCR text from a preceding recipe bleeds into the top of the document, earns maximum position bonus, and outscores the real title that appears in the middle or lower half.
    67→
    68→### Specific code change
    69→
    70→```typescript
    71→// BEFORE (lines 256-266):
    72→const relativePosition = candidate.position / lines.length;
    73→const positionBonus = relativePosition < 0.5
    74→  ? 0.15 * (1 - relativePosition * 2)
    75→  : 0;
    76→// ...
    77→const score = rawScore + positionBonus + allCapsBonus;
    78→
    79→// AFTER — multiplicative tiebreaker:
    80→const relativePosition = candidate.position / lines.length;
    81→const positionFactor = relativePosition < 0.5
    82→  ? 1.0 + 0.12 * (1 - relativePosition * 2)
    83→  : 1.0;
    84→// ...
    85→const score = rawScore * positionFactor + allCapsBonus;
    86→```
    87→
    88→### Why multiplicative
    89→
    90→A multiplicative factor amplifies existing signal strength rather than replacing it:
    91→
    92→| Candidate | rawScore | Additive (+0.15) | Multiplicative (×1.12) |
    93→|-----------|----------|-------------------|----------------------|
    94→| Garbled fragment (pos 0) | 0.03 | 0.03 + 0.15 = **0.18** | 0.03 × 1.12 = **0.034** |
    95→| Real title (pos 0.4) | 0.12 | 0.12 + 0.03 = 0.15 | 0.12 × 1.02 = **0.122** |
    96→
    97→With additive scoring, the garbled fragment wins (0.18 > 0.15). With multiplicative, the real title wins (0.122 > 0.034). The position signal becomes a tiebreaker between candidates of similar semantic quality, not a substitute for semantic quality.
    98→
    99→### Expected impact
   100→
   101→- **FINNISH MILK FLATBREADS:** "DAT FLATBREADS" (early, low semantic score) can no longer beat "FINNISH MILK FLATBREADS" (mid-document, high semantic score + ALL_CAPS bonus).
   102→- **MIXED SEED CRISPBREAD:** "sheet in to heat up..." (early, low title-similarity) can no longer beat "MIXED SEED CRISPBREAD" (line 18, strong title-similarity + ALL_CAPS bonus).
   103→- **SAFFRON WHEAT BUNS:** Garbled early text at lines 1–28 no longer gets enough position boost to overcome the semantic + ALL_CAPS advantage of the real title at lines 54–55.
   104→
   105→---
   106→
   107→## Change 3: Strengthen Garble Detection
   108→
   109→**Addresses:** Failures #3, #4, #5
   110→
   111→### Root cause
   112→
   113→`isLikelyGarbled` uses vowel ratio and a 25-character-limited lowercase-start heuristic. Three categories of OCR noise slip through:
   114→
   115→1. **Long lowercase fragments:** "sheet in to heat up. arge bowl and mix" (38 chars) passes because the lowercase-start check only fires under 25 chars.
   116→2. **ALL_CAPS truncations mixed with real words:** "DAT FLATBREADS" passes because "DAT" has normal vowel ratio.
   117→3. **Joined garbled lines:** When a NON_TITLE_WORD like "BUNS" joins with garbled neighbours ("BUNS ssekart"), the combined string passes all checks.
   118→
   119→### Change 3a: Remove 25-char limit on lowercase-start detection
   120→
   121→Recipe titles virtually never start with a lowercase letter. The 25-char limit was overly cautious.
   122→
   123→```typescript
   124→// BEFORE (line 100):
   125→if (text.length < 25 && /^[a-z]/.test(text.trim()) && !text.trim().endsWith(".")) {
   126→  return true;
   127→}
   128→
   129→// AFTER — no length restriction:
   130→if (/^[a-z]/.test(text.trim())) {
   131→  return true;
   132→}
   133→```
   134→
   135→**Lines this now catches across all inputs:**
   136→- Failure #4: "sheet in to heat up. arge bowl and mix" — starts with 's'
   137→- Failure #5: "ssekart" (line 2), "alled out into names." (line 3), "favoured by" (line 5), "the julgalt." (line 6), "ly named as..." (line 7), "cover the bun" (line 8)
   138→- Various garbled lines across all OCR inputs that start lowercase
   139→
   140→**Safety:** Verified against all 6 test inputs — no real title starts with lowercase. English and Polish recipe titles always start with an uppercase letter.
   141→
   142→### Change 3b: Detect mid-text sentence boundaries
   143→
   144→Body text fragments spliced from multiple sentences contain periods followed by lowercase letters mid-text. Titles never do.
   145→
   146→```typescript
   147→// NEW check in isLikelyGarbled, before final return false:
   148→const trimmed = text.trim();
   149→if (/\.\s+[a-zA-Z]/.test(trimmed) && !trimmed.endsWith(")")) {
   150→  return true;
   151→}
   152→```
   153→
   154→The `!endsWith(")")` guard prevents false positives on titles like "CHEESE (VARIATION D)" where punctuation is structural.
   155→
   156→**Lines this catches:**
   157→- "sheet in to heat up. arge bowl and mix" — ". a" mid-text (defence in depth with 3a)
   158→- "alled out into names." would be caught by 3a already, but similar patterns in future inputs are caught here
   159→
   160→### Change 3c: Filter mixed-case garbled words in multi-word candidates
   161→
   162→When building multi-line joins, garbled fragments can piggyback on valid words. Detect candidates where a word looks like an OCR truncation: a lowercase word ≤2 characters that isn't a common English short word.
   163→
   164→```typescript
   165→// NEW check in isLikelyGarbled, for multi-word candidates:
   166→const words = text.trim().split(/\s+/);
   167→if (words.length >= 2) {
   168→  const commonShort2 = new Set([
   169→    "a", "i", "of", "or", "to", "in", "on", "is", "it", "an",
   170→    "as", "at", "by", "do", "go", "if", "no", "so", "up", "we",
   171→  ]);
   172→  const hasGarbledWord = words.some(
   173→    (w) => /^[a-z]/.test(w) && w.replace(/[^a-z]/g, "").length <= 2 &&
   174→      !commonShort2.has(w.toLowerCase())
   175→  );
   176→  if (hasGarbledWord) {
   177→    return true;
   178→  }
   179→}
   180→```
   181→
   182→**Lines this catches:**
   183→- Any multi-word join containing lowercase truncated fragments like "ng", "ır", "nto"
   184→
   185→### Expected impact of Change 3 combined
   186→
   187→Most garbled lines in the FINNISH, MIXED SEED, and SAFFRON inputs are filtered before they ever reach the embedding stage. This:
   188→1. Reduces the candidate pool to mostly legitimate titles
   189→2. Prevents garbled text from consuming embedding calls (performance win)
   190→3. Works synergistically with Change 2 — even if a garbled fragment slips through, it can't win on position alone
   191→
   192→---
   193→
   194→## Change 4: "First Structural ALL_CAPS Heading" Bonus
   195→
   196→**Addresses:** Failures #3, #4, #5 — specifically the pattern where the real title is a prominent ALL_CAPS heading mid-document
   197→
   198→### Root cause
   199→
   200→After reducing the position bonus (Change 2), mid-document titles rely on semantic score + the existing 0.08 ALL_CAPS bonus. For ambiguous cases where OCR corruption is moderate and the title is deep in the document (like SAFFRON WHEAT BUNS at position 0.71), this may not be enough margin.
   201→
   202→Cookbook scans have a strong structural convention: **the first ALL_CAPS multi-word heading that isn't garbled is almost always a recipe title**. The algorithm should exploit this structural signal directly.
   203→
   204→### Specific code change
   205→
   206→After `buildCandidates`, before the scoring loop:
   207→
   208→```typescript
   209→// Find the first ALL_CAPS candidate with ≥2 words where every word has ≥4 letters.
   210→// The ≥4-letter requirement filters OCR truncations like "DAT", "FRON", "BAS".
   211→const firstStructuralHeading = candidates.find(
   212→  (c) =>
   213→    isAllCaps(c.text) &&
   214→    wordCount(c.text) >= 2 &&
   215→    c.text
   216→      .trim()
   217→      .split(/\s+/)
   218→      .every((w) => w.replace(/[^A-Z]/g, "").length >= 4)
   219→);
   220→```
   221→
   222→In the scoring loop, add a bonus for this candidate:
   223→
   224→```typescript
   225→const structuralBonus =
   226→  firstStructuralHeading && candidate === firstStructuralHeading ? 0.10 : 0;
   227→
   228→const score = rawScore * positionFactor + allCapsBonus + structuralBonus;
   229→```
   230→
   231→### Why ≥4 letters per word
   232→
   233→OCR truncation artifacts are typically 1–3 letter fragments of longer words. Requiring every word to have ≥4 letters filters:
   234→- "DAT" (3 letters) in "DAT FLATBREADS" — prevented from getting structural bonus
   235→- "FRON" (4 letters, passes) in "FRON WHEAT BUNS" — borderline, but "FRON" has 4 letters
   236→
   237→Actually, "FRON" is exactly 4 letters. Let me verify the SAFFRON input: line 29 is "FRON WHEAT BUNS (VARIATION 1)". After stripping non-alpha: "FRON" = 4 letters. This would pass. But the word "(VARIATION" with number "1)" — the `startsWithNumber` check won't catch this since the line doesn't start with a number. However, "1)" is a non-alpha word that would have 0 alpha chars — failing the ≥4 check. So "FRON WHEAT BUNS (VARIATION 1)" would fail the all-words-≥4-letters check because "1)" has 0 alpha letters.
   238→
   239→Wait, the word splitting: "FRON WHEAT BUNS (VARIATION 1)". Split on spaces: ["FRON", "WHEAT", "BUNS", "(VARIATION", "1)"]. After stripping non-alpha: "FRON"→4, "WHEAT"→5, "BUNS"→4, "VARIATION"→9, ""→0. The "1)" maps to 0 alpha letters → fails ≥4 → the whole line fails the structural heading check.
   240→
   241→So "FRON WHEAT BUNS (VARIATION 1)" does NOT get the structural heading bonus. The first qualifying heading would be "SAFFRON WHEAT BUNS WITH QUARK" (if it's a single-line candidate) or the 2-line join "SAFFRON WHEAT BUNS WITH QUARK / COTTAGE CHEESE (VARIATION D)". Let's check: "(VARIATION" → 9 alpha letters, "D)" → 1 alpha letter → fails. So the join also fails.
   242→
   243→The single-line candidate "SAFFRON WHEAT BUNS WITH QUARK" — words: ["SAFFRON"→7, "WHEAT"→5, "BUNS"→4, "WITH"→4, "QUARK"→5] — all ≥4 → passes. This gets the structural heading bonus.
   244→
   245→For "FINNISH MILK FLATBREADS": words ["FINNISH"→7, "MILK"→4, "FLATBREADS"→10] — all ≥4 → passes. Gets bonus.
   246→
   247→For "DAT FLATBREADS": words ["DAT"→3, "FLATBREADS"→10] — "DAT" has 3 letters → fails. No structural bonus.
   248→
   249→For "MIXED SEED CRISPBREAD": words ["MIXED"→5, "SEED"→4, "CRISPBREAD"→10] — all ≥4 → passes. Gets bonus.
   250→
   251→### Expected impact
   252→
   253→| Case | First Structural Heading | Bonus |
   254→|------|--------------------------|-------|
   255→| ARAYES SHRAK | "ARAYES SHRAK" (both ≥5) | +0.10 |
   256→| FINNISH MILK FLATBREADS | "FINNISH MILK FLATBREADS" (not "DAT FLATBREADS") | +0.10 |
   257→| MIXED SEED CRISPBREAD | "MIXED SEED CRISPBREAD" | +0.10 |
   258→| SAFFRON WHEAT BUNS | "SAFFRON WHEAT BUNS WITH QUARK" | +0.10 |
   259→| OVERNIGHT STRAIGHT PIZZA DOUGH | "OVERNIGHT STRAIGHT PIZZA DOUGH" | +0.10 |
   260→
   261→This bonus stacks with the ALL_CAPS bonus (+0.08), giving the first structural heading a total of +0.18 on top of its semantic score — a decisive advantage.
   262→
   263→---
   264→
   265→## Combined Scoring Formula
   266→
   267→```typescript
   268→const rawScore = titleSim - Math.max(headerSim, noiseSim);
   269→
   270→const relativePosition = candidate.position / lines.length;
   271→const positionFactor = relativePosition < 0.5
   272→  ? 1.0 + 0.12 * (1 - relativePosition * 2)
   273→  : 1.0;
   274→
   275→const allCapsBonus = isAllCaps(candidate.text) &&
   276→  candidate.text.replace(/[^a-zA-Z]/g, "").length >= 4
   277→  ? 0.08
   278→  : 0;
   279→
   280→const structuralBonus =
   281→  firstStructuralHeading && candidate === firstStructuralHeading
   282→  ? 0.10
   283→  : 0;
   284→
   285→const score = rawScore * positionFactor + allCapsBonus + structuralBonus;
   286→```
   287→
   288→---
   289→
   290→## Projected Results
   291→
   292→| Test Case | Current | After Changes | Key Changes |
   293→|---|---|---|---|
   294→| ARAYES SHRAK | FAIL ("ARAYES") | PASS | Change 1: dedup keeps "ARAYES SHRAK" over "ARAYES" |
   295→| Baked Eggs with Feta... | FAIL (spurious " + ") | PASS | Change 1: dedup keeps full title; partial + fragment both eliminated as substrings |
   296→| FINNISH MILK FLATBREADS + FINNISH POTATO FLATBREADS | FAIL ("DAT FLATBREADS") | PASS | Change 2: position can't carry weak semantic. Change 4: structural bonus skips "DAT" (3 letters). Real titles get structural + caps bonuses |
   297→| MIXED SEED CRISPBREAD | FAIL ("sheet in to...") | PASS | Change 3a: lowercase-start filter catches "sheet in to...". Change 4: structural bonus for real title |
   298→| OVERNIGHT STRAIGHT PIZZA DOUGH | PASS | PASS | No regression — position factor (1.12x) still helps, caps + structural bonuses reinforce |
   299→| SAFFRON WHEAT BUNS WITH QUARK... | FAIL ("BUNS ssekart + ...") | PASS | Change 3a/3c: garble filters eliminate lines 1–28. Change 2: reduced position stops remaining noise. Change 4: structural bonus for "SAFFRON WHEAT BUNS WITH QUARK" |
   300→
   301→**Confidence:** High for all 5 fixes. The SAFFRON case has the most moving parts but benefits from all four changes working together: garble detection eliminates most competitors, reduced position bonus removes the unfair advantage of remaining noise, and the structural heading bonus directly rewards the first clean ALL_CAPS heading.
   302→
   303→---
   304→
   305→## Risk Assessment
   306→
   307→**Regression risk for OVERNIGHT STRAIGHT PIZZA DOUGH (currently passing):**
   308→This title is at position 0 and ALL_CAPS. Under the new formula: rawScore × 1.12 + 0.08 (caps) + 0.10 (structural) = rawScore × 1.12 + 0.18. Under the old formula: rawScore + 0.15 (position) + 0.08 (caps) = rawScore + 0.23. For any rawScore > 0, the new formula gives rawScore × 0.12 + 0.18 vs rawScore × 0 + 0.23 = net change of rawScore × 0.12 - 0.05. For rawScore > 0.42 this is an improvement; for lower rawScores the structural bonus compensates. No regression expected.
   309→
   310→**Edge cases to watch:**
   311→- Pages with no ALL_CAPS text at all (e.g., the Baked Eggs input) — the structural heading bonus simply doesn't apply; scoring falls back to semantic + position tiebreaker, which is the correct behavior for mixed-case titles.
   312→- Very long OCR documents — the 25-candidate cap and early garble filtering keep performance bounded.
   313→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

content.thinking:
Now I understand the current code and the improvement plan. Let me implement the four changes:

1. **Change 1**: Invert substring deduplication (lines 281-289)
2. **Change 2**: Reduce position bonus from additive to multiplicative
3. **Change 3**: Strengthen garble detection (3a, 3b, 3c)
4. **Change 4**: "First Structural ALL_CAPS Heading" Bonus

Let me now read the test file to understand what tests exist.

content.text: Now let me read the test file before making changes.

content.thinking:
Now I have a clear understanding of everything. Let me implement the four changes from the improvement plan:

**Change 1**: Invert substring deduplication (lines 281-289)
- Remove shorter substrings, keep longer candidates

**Change 2**: Position bonus from additive to multiplicative
- `positionFactor = 1.0 + 0.12 * (1 - relativePosition * 2)` instead of additive `+0.15`
- `score = rawScore * positionFactor + allCapsBonus`

**Change 3**: Strengthen garble detection
- 3a: Remove 25-char limit on lowercase-start detection


- 3b: Detect mid-text sentence boundaries (`. ` followed by letter)
- 3c: Filter mixed-case garbled words in multi-word candidates

**Change 4**: "First Structural ALL_CAPS Heading" Bonus
- Find first ALL_CAPS candidate with ≥2 words where every word has ≥4 alpha letters
- Give it +0.10 structural bonus

Now I'm checking the test cases to verify these changes won't break anything, starting with the non-caps titles test case.

For the test with "Pasta Carbonara" and "Tiramisu Classico" - both are mixed case so no structural bonus applies. The trickier one is the short lines test where "This is a longer line..." starts with uppercase and passes the hard filters, giving it a neutral score that I need to calculate through.

Now I'm working through what happens if I remove the 25-character limit on the lowercase-start filter. The Polish text "śmietanowym z pieczarkami" starts with a lowercase character, so it would get caught by this stricter filter even though it's part of a valid joined title. That's a problem because the test expects this line to contribute to the final result. - starts uppercase → passes
   - 3-line join: "Makaron z sosem pomidorowym i bazylią" - starts uppercase → passes

Now I'm checking the garbled words logic. The word "i" is a single letter that wouldn't be in the common short words list, so it would trigger the garbled word filter based on its length.

But "i" is actually a valid Polish word meaning "and" — it's used in the test case itself. If the garbled word check flags it, the multi-line title test would fail. I need to figure out whether to add "i" to the common short words set or handle Polish-specific words differently.

Looking closer at the logic, the same problem applies to "z" (a Polish preposition) — it's lowercase, single character, and not in commonShort2, so it would also get flagged as garbled. This means phrases like "Makaron z sosem pomidorowym" would fail the garbled word check if treated as multi-word candidates, which would break the Polish tests entirely.

I need to rethink this approach — either add Polish single-character prepositions like "w", "z", "i", "o", "u" to the commonShort2 set, or find a different way to detect OCR truncations without catching legitimate short words in other languages. The original spec mentions detecting fragments like "ng", "ır", "nto" in multi-word joins, so maybe I should focus on patterns that look more distinctly like truncation artifacts rather than just any short lowercase word. Looking at this test case, I see the issue—"z" and "i" are single-character Polish words that should be allowed in title joins, but my current fragment detection would reject them since they're not in the commonShort2 set. I need to add these Polish short words to avoid false positives. I'm seeing that several Polish recipe titles with prepositions like "w" and "z" would also fail the validation rule, so I need to expand the commonShort2 set to include these common Polish words rather than treating them as OCR artifacts.

The real issue is distinguishing between legitimate short words and actual truncation fragments—the key difference is that real words have vowels, while OCR truncations like "ng", "ır", or "nto" are just consonant clusters. So maybe the check should target lowercase words of 2-3 characters with no vowels at all, which would catch the actual fragments while letting legitimate short words through. The key insight is that these are word-END fragments: "ng" from "baking", "nto" from "into". They don't have vowels (or only consonants).

The safest approach that won't break Polish is to add Polish common short words to the set. Let me add: "a", "i", "o", "u", "w", "z" and also perhaps "na", "do", "od", "po", "ze", "we", "co", "to", "że", "ja" etc.

But this is getting complex. Let me just add the essential Polish single-character prepositions/conjunctions that are clearly used: w, z, i, a, o, u.

Actually, I think the simpler approach is to require that the short word has no vowels to be considered garbled. "ng" has no vowels, "nto" has one vowel but "n" starts it and it's a clear fragment. "ır" has no English vowels.

But "i" (Polish "and") and "w" (Polish "in") and "z" (Polish "from/with") are all vowel-containing or single consonants that ARE real words. Since the app already supports Polish based on the references in the code, I should add these common Polish single-letter words to the short words set: "i", "w", "z", and "a". Looking at the common short words list, I see that "i" is already included, so Polish "i" (and) would pass the filter. But "w" and "z" aren't there, which means they'd be incorrectly flagged as garbled text. I need to add both of these single-letter Polish prepositions to the commonShort2 list to handle them properly. I see that "i" is already in the commonShort2 set, but "w" and "z" are missing—those are the only problematic short words in the Polish text. Adding both of them would resolve the issue.

I'm also considering whether other OCR fragments like "nw" could slip through, but those wouldn't be in the common set anyway so they'd still get caught. Let me verify the other lowercase words in the second example: "pomidorowym" has 11 characters so it won't trigger the length check, and "sosem" and "bazylią" are similarly too long to be flagged as problematic short words. 7 chars > 2, NOT caught.

So only "z" and "i" are problematic. "i" is already in the set, and adding "w" and "z" fixes it.

But wait, there's also the 2-line join test "Kurczak w sosie śmietanowym z pieczarkami" from the "joins multi-line title (2 lines)" test. Words: ["Kurczak", "w", "sosie", "śmietanowym", "z", "pieczarkami"]. "w" (1 char) and "z" (1 char) would both need to be in commonShort2.

Actually there's another issue - "śmietanowym" - it starts with "ś" which is lowercase. The regex that removes non-ASCII characters would strip the "ś", leaving "mietanowym" which has 10 chars > 2, so that's fine. "pieczarkami" starts with "p" (lowercase), 11 chars > 2, also fine.

So I should add "w" and "z" to commonShort2 to support Polish. Though I'm noticing the improvement plan doesn't mention this issue and doesn't test against the Polish unit tests—it analyzes real OCR inputs from English recipe books. The existing unit tests include Polish cases, so I think the right approach is to add "w" and "z" to commonShort2.

Now looking at how Change 3a affects the Polish tests: "śmietanowym z pieczarkami" starts with 'ś' (lowercase), so it would be marked as garbled with the no-length-limit change. But the 2-line join "Kurczak w sosie śmietanowym z pieczarkami" starts with 'K' (uppercase), so it passes. The single lowercase line gets filtered but the join survives, which is fine. For the 3-line join test, "pomidorowym" starts with 'p' (lowercase), so it would be garbled with Change 3a.

Now with "i bazylią" - it starts with 'i' (lowercase), so that would also be garbled. However, the 3-line join "Makaron z sosem pomidorowym i bazylią" starts with 'M' (uppercase) and passes. The 2-line join "Makaron z sosem pomidorowym" also starts with 'M' and passes, and the mock embed would match it against the expected result "makaron z sosem pomidorowym i bazylią" with high title similarity since the 3-line join contains that full phrase.

But now I'm realizing both the 2-line and 3-line joins would be candidates, and when I score them, the shorter "Makaron z sosem" doesn't actually contain the full phrase "makaron z sosem pomidorowym i bazylią" as a substring, so it gets a neutral score instead of high similarity. " → includes "makaron z sosem"? Wait, the check is:
     ```
     const isTitle = titleWords.some((w) => lower.includes(w.toLowerCase()));
     ```
     titleWords = ["makaron z sosem pomidorowym i bazylią"]. "makaron z sosem pomidorowym".includes("makaron z sosem pomidorowym i bazylią") → false. So this also gets neutral.
   - "Makaron z sosem pomidorowym i bazylią" → "makaron z sosem pomidorowym i bazylią".includes("makaron z sosem pomidorowym i bazylią") → true! Gets [0.9, 0.1, 0].

   So the 3-line join correctly gets the highest score. Good.

   But wait - with Change 3c and "z" added to commonShort2, does the 3-line join pass? "Makaron z sosem pomidorowym i bazylią": "z" is in commonShort2 (after my fix), "i" is in commonShort2. So Change 3c would NOT flag this candidate. Good.

   Now I'm checking whether other tests might break. Looking at the "two titles deep in text returns both joined" test, I need to verify if "200g pasta" would pass the number check—it starts with digits, so it should be filtered out.

Now I'm examining the remaining candidates like "Pasta Carbonara" and "Tiramisu Classico"—both start with uppercase letters, so they pass the lowercase-word filter. Neither is a substring of the other, so deduplication doesn't remove them. The test should still return both titles joined together.

For the multi-title scenario with "First Recipe" and "Second Recipe", both score equally well since they're structured the same way, and without any ALL_CAPS entries, the guard keeps all high-scoring candidates. Now I'm checking the multi-title guard logic more carefully—if two or more ALL_CAPS titles are selected, keep only those; if exactly one ALL_CAPS is selected...

Actually, let me trace through the "preserves all-caps titles" test case where "CHOCOLATE CAKE" appears. This is ALL_CAPS, and both words meet the length threshold, so it qualifies as a valid title candidate.

It gets the structural bonus of +0.10 since it's positioned at the top. The raw score calculation gives us 0.8 after subtracting the header similarity from the title similarity. With the document being short (2-3 lines), the position factor maxes out at 1.12, and we're also applying an all-caps bonus of 0.08 on top of that.

So the final score comes to 0.8 × 1.12 + 0.08 + 0.10 = 1.076. "Ingredients" scores much lower at -0.8 since it's mostly header similarity with minimal title match, and anything starting with a number gets filtered out anyway. "CHOCOLATE CAKE" clearly wins here.

Now I'm moving on to evaluate Change 3b.

I'm checking for garbled text by looking for periods followed by lowercase letters mid-sentence—like "sheet in to heat up. arge bowl and mix" which has that telltale ". a" pattern. "CHOCOLATE CAKE" doesn't have this issue. I should also verify the other test cases don't trigger false positives on this check.

Let me trace through Change 1 more carefully with the existing test where we have "Apple Pie\nIngredients\n200g flour" and need to make sure the algorithm doesn't over-greedily combine the title with the header section. Checking if "200g flour" gets caught by the number filter—yes, it starts with a digit so that's filtered out. So my candidates are "Apple Pie", "Apple Pie Ingredients", and "Ingredients". Now I need to verify whether "Ingredients" itself matches the metadata patterns, which check for things like "SERVES", "PREP TIME", "BULK FERMENTATION"—and it doesn't seem to match any of those.

With the mock embeddings where title words are ["apple pie"] and header words are ["ingredients"], I'm scoring each candidate: "Apple Pie" gets a strong match on title, "Apple Pie Ingredients" matches both but dilutes the score, and "Ingredients" only matches the header words, giving it a negative raw score.

Now I'm applying the threshold filter—the best score is "Apple Pie" at 0.896 after accounting for position and bonus factors, which sets the threshold at 0.717. This means "Apple Pie Ingredients" with its near-zero raw score won't make the cut.

For the dedup logic with the new substring removal rule, "Apple Pie Ingredients" would normally be removed since it's longer and contains "Apple Pie", but it's already been filtered out by the threshold, so the dedup step doesn't come into play here. Let me check how this affects the tests that prioritize titles over section headers.

Now I'm working through a Polish recipe example with "Składniki" (ingredients header), "Pierogi Ruskie" (the dish title), and ingredient items. The header gets a negative score from the word weights, while "Pierogi Ruskie" scores positively at 0.8, and with its position near the top of the list, the position factor boosts it to around 1.06 multiplier.

The ingredient lines like "200g mąki" and "3 ziemniaki" are filtered out because they start with numbers. When I try combining "Składniki" with "Pierogi Ruskie" across lines, the mixed word weights cancel out to zero, so "Pierogi Ruskie" clearly wins as the best title candidate.

Now I'm testing another case with "Kurczak w sosie śmietanowym z pieczarkami" to see how the deduplication logic handles it. The full title scores much higher at 0.896, which after applying the threshold multiplier gives 0.717—well above what the shorter "Kurczak w sosie" candidate would achieve, so the longer match clearly wins out.

But I'm realizing there's an important edge case: if both candidates somehow scored above the threshold, the deduplication logic becomes critical. The old approach would incorrectly remove the longer string when it contained the shorter one, but the new dedup correctly removes only the shorter substring, preserving the more complete result. the OLD test was passing before. Does the new dedup change affect it? In the current code, both would need to be above threshold for dedup to matter. In this test, "Kurczak w sosie" has rawScore -0.4 so it would be below threshold. So dedup doesn't matter for this test case either way.

Now let me think about the "does not over-greedily join title with header" test more carefully with Change 1:
- If "Apple Pie" and "Apple Pie Ingredients" were both above threshold, Change 1 would KEEP "Apple Pie Ingredients" (longer) and REMOVE "Apple Pie" (shorter). This would be wrong!
- But as I showed above, "Apple Pie Ingredients" never gets above threshold. So this is fine.

Actually, could a join that includes a header word still score above threshold? In this test, "Apple Pie Ingredients" gets rawScore=0, positionFactor=1.12, allCapsBonus=0, structuralBonus=0, for a final score of 0, which is well below the 0.717 threshold. Even if the mock embedding gave a higher score to the joined candidate, in practice real embeddings would penalize combining a title with a header word, and the mock embed gives rawScore=0 for combined title+header words anyway. So Change 1 should be safe across all existing tests.

Now I'm checking the "two titles deep in text returns both joined" test with Change 1 to see how "Pasta Carbonara" and "Tiramisu Classico" behave. - neither is a substring of the other → dedup doesn't change anything.

   And "single-recipe page still returns one title":
   ```
   "Tomato Soup\nIngredients\n4 tomatoes\n1 cup cream\nSalt"
   ```
   - "1 cup cream" → starts with digit? Wait: "1 cup cream". `startsWithNumber` = `^\s*\d` → yes, starts with "1" → filtered.
   - "4 tomatoes" → starts with "4" → filtered.
   - "Salt" - single word, 4 chars. Is "salt" in NON_TITLE_WORDS? No. passesHardFilters: length 4, not ingredient, not number, not metadata, not garbled. OK, it's a candidate.
   - "Tomato Soup" - gets [0.9, 0.1, 0] with a raw score of 0.8, making it the strongest candidate. "Salt" scores much lower at -0.4, and "Ingredients" is nearly disqualified at -0.8. Now I'm calculating the final threshold by factoring in position, capitalization, and structural bonuses for "Tomato Soup" at the start of the document.

   So my implementation plan is to make four key changes: invert the deduplication logic to keep the highest-scoring duplicate, convert the position bonus from additive to multiplicative, remove the character limit on the lowercase-start check, and add checks for sentence boundaries and garbled words while expanding the common short words list for Polish support.

   Now I'm reconsidering the garbled word detection. Looking at that test case with the burst of short lines, I need to be careful—"pg 42" starts with lowercase 'p', so without the length limit it would be flagged as garbled, but it's actually part of a legitimate page reference. Let me think through which lines should actually be filtered here. Let me trace through the `findBurstEnd` function with this data structure, checking each line against the burst detection conditions—whether it's under 20 characters and matches the garbled text pattern. it anyway. At i=3, the line "This is a longer line that triggers burst end detection" is long enough to stop the burst, so I check if it passes the filters—it's 54 characters, not garbled, and doesn't contain ". ". Now I'm examining whether it has any lowercase short words not in the common list, starting with "is".

"is" is actually all lowercase and in the commonShort2 set, so it's fine. Same with "a". The longer words like "line", "that", and "end" are over 2 characters, so they don't trigger the filter either.

Now let me trace through the actual scoring: "This is a longer line that triggers burst end detection" passes the hard filters and becomes a candidate. When I run it against the mock embedding with titleWords=["beef stew"] and headerWords=["ingredients"], it doesn't match either, giving it a neutral score of 0.3 - 0.7 = -0.4. Meanwhile, "Beef Stew" scores much higher at 0.8, so the burst-skipping logic still works as intended.

But I should double-check whether that longer line could accidentally score above threshold and break things. At position 3 out of 7 lines, the relative position is about 0.43, which gives a position factor of roughly 1.017. With the raw score of -0.4, the final score comes to about -0.407—clearly negative and not a threat. "Beef Stew" at position 4 would score higher, so the ranking holds up.

Now let me verify how the algorithm handles all-caps titles. For the "CHOCOLATE CAKE" test case, the candidates include the title itself at position 0, "Ingredients" at position 1, and a 2-line join combining both. The all-caps handling should preserve the title correctly. The "Ingredients" section gets filtered out because it contains lowercase letters, so "CHOCOLATE CAKE" becomes the structural heading with a bonus that pushes its score to 1.076. Now I'm ready to implement the code changes—updating the garble detection logic to remove the character limit, add sentence boundary checks, and incorporate Polish short word validation, then move on to the next section.

Writing the garble detection function... I'm checking for several more patterns that indicate corrupted text: lines starting with lowercase letters (recipe titles should be capitalized), sentence fragments with periods and spaces in the middle, and multi-word candidates containing garbled OCR artifacts like uncommon short words.

Now I'm looking at the scoring logic to identify the first structurally valid heading...

Refining the position-based weighting...

Now I'm adding bonuses for all-caps text since recipe books typically use that for titles and section headers, plus a structural bonus if this is the first valid all-caps heading we've found. Then I'm combining these with the position factor to calculate the final score.

For deduplication, I'm filtering out shorter titles that are substrings of longer ones to keep the more complete version.

lines: -  // Line starts with lowercase and is a short sentence fragment — OCR corruption indicator
+  // Recipe titles never start with a lowercase letter
+  // Mid-text sentence boundary (". " followed by a letter) — body text fragment spliced from multiple sentences
+  // Multi-word candidate containing a garbled OCR fragment: a short lowercase word that isn't a known short word

content.content: The file /Users/eryk.napierala/Projects/fasola/lib/text-classifier/title-extractor.ts has been updated successfully.

lines: +  // Find the first ALL_CAPS candidate with ≥2 words where every word has ≥4 alpha letters.
+  // This structural signal identifies the first clean recipe title heading in cookbook scans.
const scored: Array<{ text: string; position: number; score: number }> = [];
-    // Position bonus: up to +0.15 for candidates in the first half of the document
+    // Position factor: multiplicative tiebreaker — amplifies existing signal, doesn't replace it
// ALL_CAPS bonus: recipe books use ALL_CAPS for titles and section headings
+    // Structural heading bonus: first clean ALL_CAPS heading is almost always the recipe title
+    const score = rawScore * positionFactor + allCapsBonus + structuralBonus;
scored.push({ text: candidate.text, position: candidate.position, score });
